issues.title: Multi-rank / back edges route straight through intervening node boxes — reproducible in the project's own state-machine.mmd fixture
issues.description: src/geometry/index.ts routeElbow() (called from layout()'s per-edge routing loop in src/layout/index.ts) computes an edge's elbow waypoints purely from the source and destination NodeBox centers (pickSides() + a straight midpoint bisector) with zero awareness of any other node that may sit between them, and without using dagre's own multi-rank edge waypoints (dagre normally emits bend points that route around intervening ranks/dummy nodes; this project discards them and recomputes a naive 2-segment elbow instead). VERIFIED against the project's own bundled fixture fixtures/state-machine.mmd: the `errored -->|retry| loading` back-edge (a state machine retry loop, `graph TD` with a cycle) is routed as `M 307.5 370 L 307.5 278.5 L 104 278.5 L 104 187`, whose horizontal segment at y=278.5 passes exactly through (104,278.5) — the geometric CENTER of the 'Response OK?' diamond (polygon center 104,278.5) — cutting the diamond node in half. Rendered to PNG (test/artifacts/state-machine.png) and in the interactive DOM renderer (test/artifacts/demo-statemachine.html, screenshot 08-statemachine-interactive-edge-overlap-bug.png) the defect is clearly visible: the 'retry' edge label is visually clipped to 'etry' by the node card, and 2-3 edges cross directly through the card's interior. A minimal constructed repro confirms it is NOT cycle-specific but a general multi-rank routing gap: `flowchart TD / A-->B-->C / A-->C` (test/artifacts/skiplevel.mmd) routes A-->C as a dead-straight `M 73.25 50 L 73.25 212`, overlapping node B's box by ~1.75px. An automated bounding-box overlap scan across every rendered SVG in this round found overlaps in: cycle.svg (1), skiplevel.svg (1), a 35-node adversarial graph with skip-edges (17 overlaps), and — critically — state-machine.svg / the format-inferred render of the same fixture (3 overlaps), confirming this is not only a contrived adversarial case but affects a real-world example the project ships. Because renderSvg/renderPng/renderAscii/renderHtml/mount() all share routeEdge()/routeElbow(), the defect is identical across every output format (confirmed also in ci-pipeline.md ASCII output, see TEST-005). This directly contradicts the plan's core premise (orthogonal elbow edges anchored to card borders, 'beautiful' non-overlapping diagrams) for any flowchart containing a cycle or a skip-level edge — both very common real-world flowchart patterns (state machines, retry/rollback loops, early-exit branches).
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE (non-trivial, core geometry work). Make routeElbow() (and routeCurved()) aware of intervening node boxes: either (a) feed through dagre's own edge.points (the bend/dummy-node waypoints dagre computes for multi-rank edges) instead of discarding them in src/layout/index.ts's per-edge loop, adapting them to the border-anchored elbow style, or (b) after computing the naive 2-point elbow, check the turn segment(s) against every other node's NodeBox (excluding the edge's own endpoints) and offset the turn coordinate (e.g. nudge the midpoint past the nearest occupied lane, mirroring how dagre already keeps nodes non-overlapping) when a collision is detected. Add a regression test in test/geometry.test.ts and/or test/layout.test.ts that lays out a 3-node vertical chain with a skip edge (A->B->C, A->C) and asserts the routed A->C path's bounding box does not intersect B's NodeBox, plus a cycle case (A->B->C->A) asserting the back edge does not intersect B. Re-render fixtures/state-machine.mmd after the fix and confirm visually (and via the overlap-scan approach used in this round) that the retry/give-up edges no longer cross the 'Response OK?' diamond.
issues.fix_summary: layout/index.ts now threads dagre's own interior multi-rank waypoints (only for edges dagre gave a real detour — >3 points) through geometry.routeEdge, which orthogonalizes them into a border-anchored staircase (elbow) or a rounded path (curved) via new snapWaypoints/elbowThrough/roundedPath helpers; the waypoints are stored on RoutedEdge so the inlined DOM runtime re-routes the same detour while dragging, keeping static SVG and the interactive renderer in parity (dom-runtime-parity test extended with a waypoint case). Adjacent edges (<=3 dagre points) keep the naive elbow, so their routing and all existing snapshots are byte-identical. Regression tests in layout.test.ts assert skip-level (A->B->C,A->C), a cycle back-edge, the state-machine 'retry' edge vs the 'Response OK?' diamond, and every shipped fixture leave 0 edge segments cutting through a non-endpoint node. Commit 2aa0eb9.

issues.title: Toolbar fit-to-view / zoom-in / zoom-out buttons do not respond to real mouse clicks — pointer capture on the viewport swallows the click
issues.description: In src/render/dom/runtime.ts, onPointerDown() (registered on `viewport` at line ~637: `viewport.addEventListener('pointerdown', onPointerDown)`) checks only whether the pointerdown target is inside a `.vnm-node` (drag) and otherwise unconditionally treats it as the start of a background pan: it sets `mode = 'pan'`, records `startTx/startTy`, and calls `viewport.setPointerCapture(ev.pointerId)` (lines ~544-550). Because the toolbar (`.vnm-toolbar`, appended as a child of `viewport`) is not excluded, a real user click on a toolbar button (⤢ fit-to-view, + zoom-in, − zoom-out) ALSO bubbles a pointerdown to `viewport`, which captures the pointer before the button's own `click` listener (btn.addEventListener('click', ...) at line ~236, which calls `fit()`/`zoomBy()`) can fire. The captured pointer redirects the implicit pointerup away from the button, which prevents the browser from synthesizing a `click` event on the button at all, so `fit()`/`zoomBy()` are never invoked. VERIFIED via Playwright MCP against test/artifacts/demo-light.html (CLI-exported HTML for ci-pipeline.mmd): after panning the view away from its initial transform, a REAL simulated mouse click (mouse.move + down + up, matching both real users and Playwright's page.locator(...).click()) on any of the three toolbar buttons leaves `.vnm-world`'s `style.transform` completely unchanged, while a raw `element.click()` DOM call (bypassing native pointer-event simulation) correctly resets the transform to the fitted value — proving `fit()`/`zoomBy()` themselves are correct and only the click *delivery* is broken. Contrast: the minimap's own pointerdown handler (`onMinimapDown`, line ~591) calls `ev.stopPropagation()` as its very first statement, correctly preventing this same hijack — so minimap click/drag-to-recenter works fine (verified working). The existing e2e test `e2e/exported-html.spec.ts` > 'fit-to-view sets a world transform' only asserts the resulting transform string `.toContain('scale(')`, which is true both before and after any click (the transform always contains that substring), so it cannot detect the button doing nothing — this is why the bug shipped despite a green e2e suite. I added a proper regression test to e2e/exported-html.spec.ts ('fit-to-view actually resets the transform after a real mouse click') that pans away first and asserts the click actually restores the original transform via a real mouse click; it reproducibly FAILS on the current build (see test-01.md for the exact assertion diff), and a new 'minimap drag recenters the main view' test (which passes) locks in that the minimap path is unaffected.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. Mirror the minimap's fix in onPointerDown() (src/render/dom/runtime.ts ~line 533): early-return (or stopPropagation and skip pan/drag mode) when `(ev.target as HTMLElement).closest('.vnm-toolbar')` is truthy, before checking for `.vnm-node`. The toolbar buttons' own click handlers already call `ev.stopPropagation()`, so once the viewport stops hijacking the pointerdown, the natural click should fire correctly. After the fix, `npm run test:e2e` should turn the new 'fit-to-view actually resets the transform after a real mouse click' regression test green with no other change.
issues.fix_summary: onPointerDown() in src/render/dom/runtime.ts now early-returns when the pointerdown target is inside .vnm-toolbar (via target.closest('.vnm-toolbar')), before it sets pan mode or calls setPointerCapture — mirroring the minimap's existing stopPropagation guard. The viewport no longer hijacks the pointer, so the fit/zoom buttons' own click handlers fire under real pointer events. The tester's reproducing e2e ('fit-to-view actually resets the transform after a real mouse click') now passes; the 'minimap drag recenters' e2e locks in that the minimap path is unaffected. Commit 5d466e7.

issues.title: Edges render behind node cards (DOM paint order), so dragging a node to overlap a connected neighbor visually hides the connecting edge
issues.description: In src/render/dom/runtime.ts, the edges `<svg class="vnm-edges">` element is appended to `.vnm-world` BEFORE any `.vnm-node` card divs (confirmed via `Array.from(world.children)`: svg is children[0], all `.vnm-node` divs follow). With no z-index/stacking-context override, later DOM siblings paint on top, so the SVG edge layer paints underneath every node card. VERIFIED: dragging the 'Push to main' node in test/artifacts/demo-light.html close enough to overlap 'Install deps' (its connected neighbor) makes the short connecting edge segment between them effectively disappear behind the (opaque, white-background) 'Push to main' card in the rendered page (screenshot test/screenshots/02-after-drag-node.png), even though the edge path's `d` attribute is still correctly computed and attached to the node's new border position (confirmed via getAttribute('d') before/after). This is not a functional break (the model/edges stay internally consistent, and reload/export both reflect the correct positions) but it is a real, user-visible 'my edge disappeared' illusion whenever free drag brings two connected nodes close together — plausible in normal use since there is no collision/overlap prevention on drag.
issues.proposed_solution: NEEDS-USER-DECISION (design trade-off, not a clear-cut bug). Options for the next loop to choose from: (a) accept as-is — most freeform node-drag tools allow overlap and this is a minor edge case; (b) render the edges SVG layer after (on top of) the node cards, or give edges a z-index above cards (would need `pointer-events: none` on edge paths, which already appears to be the case, to keep node drag/click working); (c) add a subtle minimum-separation nudge on drop. No action required unless the user wants the polish.
issues.fix_summary: Accepted as-is per decisions.md D5 (RESOLVED by orchestrator, 2026-07-03): edges painting under the node cards is conventional (mermaid, xplan, most diagram tools), the edge reappears when the nodes are separated, and raising edges above cards would clutter the common non-overlapping case and fight the card aesthetic. Benign, not a defect. No code change.

issues.title: Fully non-Mermaid 'garbage' input still parses to a diagram and exits 0 by default — worth confirming this is the intended lenient posture
issues.description: Per FR1's documented 'lenient by default' design, the CLI happily accepts input with zero recognizable Mermaid syntax and still exits 0. VERIFIED: rendering a two-line garbage file (`this is not mermaid at all !!!! ### ???` / `random garbage text 12345`) via `node dist/cli/index.js render garbage.mmd -o garbage.svg` produces a small SVG with two nonsense nodes labelled 'this' and 'random' (word-salvage from the tokenizer), three warnings on stderr (missing-header, two unexpected-token), and exit code 0. Only `--strict` turns this into an error (exit 1). This matches the documented contract exactly (FR1: 'Lenient by default... `--strict` turns warnings into errors') and is NOT a bug against the written spec, but it does sit in some tension with the project's own non-functional-requirements.md default ('no silent failures — surface errors clearly'): a user who mistypes a filename or pastes non-Mermaid text into the CLI without `--strict` gets a small, technically-valid-but-meaningless SVG and exit 0, with the only signal being stderr warnings that are easy to miss in a pipeline (e.g. `cmd | vnm render - -o out.svg` with stderr not being watched).
issues.proposed_solution: NEEDS-USER-DECISION (product-philosophy question, not a defect against the current spec). If the lenient-by-default posture is intentional as documented, no change needed. If not, consider a heuristic in the CLI (not the library) that escalates to a stronger stderr banner (or non-zero exit even without --strict) when a very high proportion of the input produced diagnostics relative to recognized statements (e.g. zero valid nodes/edges parsed) — a signal that the input probably is not Mermaid at all, as opposed to a Mermaid file with a few unsupported constructs.
issues.fix_summary: Per decision D6 (RESOLVED, 2026-07-03): src/cli/run.ts now checks the positioned model after layout and, when it has zero renderable nodes, writes 'error: no diagram found (input produced 0 nodes)' to stderr and returns exit code 1 — in both lenient and strict modes. Unknown constructs inside otherwise-valid mermaid still parse to >=1 node and stay lenient (exit 0 + warnings). The determination lives in the CLI only; the library parse()/layout() API is unchanged and still returns the empty model without throwing, so programmatic callers decide. New CLI (child_process) tests cover fully non-mermaid symbol-soup (exit 1 + message), empty string (exit 1), and a valid file with an unsupported `click` statement (exit 0 + ignored-statement warning). Commit c51ce39.

issues.title: ASCII/markdown renderer shows box-drawing artifacts near crossing/branching edges — likely the same routing gap as TEST-001, not yet isolated
issues.description: src/render/ascii.ts's box-drawing output for fixtures/ci-pipeline.mmd (test/artifacts/ci-pipeline.md) shows a '┼' junction character rendered immediately against the left border of the 'Lint passes?' box (line 26 of the generated fence: `┼│Lint passes? │`), and inconsistent arrowhead usage where multiple edges converge near 'Ship to prod' / 'Ship to staging' / 'Report failure' — some crossings get a clean '▶'/'◀' arrowhead into the target box, others show only a bare '┼' junction glyph with no arrowhead at all, making the direction of flow ambiguous at a glance. This was not root-caused separately in this round (time-boxed); it plausibly stems from the same PositionedModel/edge-point data as TEST-001 (the ASCII renderer consumes the same routed edges), but could also be an ASCII-specific arrowhead-placement gap in ascii.ts. Not severe enough to block shipping (the ASCII output is still structurally a recognizable flowchart), but worth a look once TEST-001 is fixed to confirm whether it self-resolves.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE (verify-after, not necessarily a standalone fix). After addressing TEST-001, re-render fixtures/ci-pipeline.mmd to markdown and check whether the '┼' junction touching 'Lint passes?' and the missing arrowheads near 'Ship to prod'/'Report failure' are gone. If they persist independently of TEST-001, investigate src/render/ascii.ts's arrowhead-placement logic for edges that cross another edge's path close to a node border.
issues.fix_summary: Root-caused as ASCII-specific, not inherited from TEST-001 (ascii.ts routes center-to-center, not off edge.points). connect() drew an edge's own elbow turn with the line-merge that upgrades any h+v overlap to `┼`, so every routed turn read as an ambiguous crossing — most visibly the `┼` jammed against the 'Lint passes?' box border, with inconsistent arrowheads near converging edges. Fixed by placing proper corner glyphs (┌┐└┘) at the two elbow joints (new cornerGlyph() + Grid.corner()) and reserving `┼` for genuine crossings of two DIFFERENT edges (corner() only upgrades to `┼` when another edge already occupies the cell). ci-pipeline now renders free of `┼` with clean corners and unambiguous arrowheads into every box; a genuine two-edge crossing still shows `┼`. Fixed in code, not documented as a limitation. Commit d69c14a.
